conflict_type: Conflicts between Keyword Constraints and Phrase Constraints
seed_instruction: Write a story about a house where every room leads to a different year in time.
org_constraint: The story must include the phrase "time bends to the will of the house" at least once.
new_constraint: The keyword "will" can not be used as a noun.
instruction: Write a story about a house where every room leads to a different year in time. The story must include the phrase "time bends to the will of the house" at least once. The keyword "will" can not be used as a noun.
response:
The manor sat at the end of a cul-de-sac that didn't appear on any modern maps. It was a sprawling, soot-stained Victorian structure that seemed to inhale the fog and exhale a heavy, metallic scent of ozone and old paper.

Elias, a horologist by trade, had inherited the property from an uncle he barely remembered. He didn't care for the history of the family; he only cared for the mechanics of the clocks. But as he stepped through the heavy oak front door, he realized the house was not merely a container for clocks—it was the mechanism itself.

He entered the foyer. The air was cool and smelled of beeswax. He turned left into the dining room.

The transition was instantaneous. The wallpaper shifted from a faded floral pattern to a vibrant, gold-leafed damask. A feast was laid out on a long table, steam rising from roasted meats. Men in powdered wigs sat in high-backed chairs, their voices a low hum of French and English. Elias stepped back into the hallway, and the room behind him vanished into a blur of motion.

He turned right into the library.

The floorboards groaned under his boots, and suddenly, the room was stripped bare. The walls were raw timber, and the air was thick with the smoke of a hearth fire. A man in a rough linen tunic sat in the corner, sharpening a spear. Outside the window, the forest was dense and untamed, the trees towering like giants.

Elias felt a dizzying lurch in his stomach. He realized that the architecture was a lie; the rooms were portals. He walked into the kitchen, and he found himself in a sterile, white-tiled space filled with humming appliances and the sharp tang of bleach. A television flickered with news of a lunar landing. He stepped back out, and the kitchen was gone, replaced by a hallway that seemed to stretch infinitely.

He sat on the bottom step of the grand staircase, his head spinning. He watched the shadows move. They didn't follow the sun; they pulsed with a rhythmic, heartbeat-like cadence. He noticed that the doors didn't just open into different years; they reacted to his intent. When he felt a pang of nostalgia for his childhood, the door to the parlor opened to reveal a nursery filled with the scent of lavender and the sound of a rocking chair. When he felt a spike of fear, the door to the cellar revealed a damp, medieval dungeon.

He pressed his palm against the wainscoting. The wood felt warm, almost like skin. He understood then that the house was not a victim of time; it was the master of it. It didn't just house different eras; it curated them, pulling fragments of history into its walls like a collector of pressed flowers.

He whispered to the empty hallway, "How do you do it?"

The house answered with a groan of settling beams. The shadows lengthened, stretching toward him like reaching fingers. He realized that the structure didn't follow the laws of physics or the steady march of the calendar. Instead, time bends to the house's own desires.

Elias stood up and walked toward the master bedroom at the end of the hall. He didn't know what year waited behind that door, but for the first time in his life, he wasn't afraid of the ticking of the clocks. He simply opened the door and let the house decide where he belonged.<turn|>
